plainMeaning: The first line of the Navkāra mantra is set like a helmet upon your head, and the second line like a fine protective cloth over your mouth.
recitation: oṁ namo arihantāṇaṁ, śiraskaṁ śirasi sthitaṁ. oṁ namo siddhāṇaṁ, mukhe mukhapaṭaṁ varaṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: Om — the sacred seed-syllable opening the salutation
wordByWord.etymology: From the praṇava oṁ; in Jain usage understood as an acronym of the five Parameṣṭhins (a+a+ā+u+m → arihanta, asarīrī/siddha, ācārya, upādhyāya, muni).
wordByWord.notes: Prefixes the Arihanta-pada here, marking it as a consecrated mantra-line to be installed on the body.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit namo, from Sanskrit namaḥ (√nam, 'to bow'); the sandhi-form before a voiced sound.
wordByWord.notes: Governs the following dative-sense genitive plural.

wordByWord.gloss: to the Arihantas (the foe-conquering omniscient ones)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit gen./dat. pl. of arihanta (Skt. arhat / ari-hanta, 'slayer of the inner foes'); -āṇaṁ is the Ardhamāgadhī plural ending.
wordByWord.notes: First Navkāra pada; here it supplies the head-piece of the armour.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit śiraska, n., 'that which pertains to the head, a helmet or head-covering', from śiras ('head') + -ka.
wordByWord.notes: Accusative; the thing being installed — the 'Namo Arihantāṇaṁ' line becomes the helmet.
Marks the bodily locus of placement (nyāsa).

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit past participle of √sthā ('to stand, remain'); neuter to agree with śiraskaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Completes the first hemistich: '[the helmet] stands placed upon the head.'

wordByWord.etymology: As above; reiterated to consecrate the second mantra-line.
wordByWord.notes: The repetition signals a fresh pada being installed on a new body-part.

wordByWord.etymology: As above; Prakrit namo, Skt. namaḥ.

wordByWord.gloss: to the Siddhas (the perfected, liberated souls)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit gen./dat. pl. of siddha (Skt. siddha, 'one who is accomplished/liberated'); -āṇaṁ Ardhamāgadhī plural.
wordByWord.notes: Second Navkāra pada; here it supplies the mouth-covering.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit locative singular of mukha ('mouth, face').
wordByWord.notes: Bodily locus paralleling śirasi in the first line.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit tatpuruṣa: mukha ('mouth') + paṭa ('cloth'); accusative neuter. The Jain ascetic's mouth-cloth.
wordByWord.notes: The 'Namo Siddhāṇaṁ' line becomes this protective cloth — a charged image, since the muhpattī is itself a mark of ahiṁsā-discipline.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit vara ('best, choice', from √vṛ, 'to choose'); neuter, qualifying mukhapaṭaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Elevates the mouth-cloth from ordinary gear to a 'choicest' adamantine covering.

literalTranslation: Om, salutation to the Arihantas — [this is] the helmet set upon the head. Om, salutation to the Siddhas — [this is] the excellent cloth over the mouth.
idiomaticTranslation: Om — reverence to the Arihantas: let that line rest as a helmet upon my head. Om — reverence to the Siddhas: let that line lie as a finest cloth across my mouth.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The armouring now begins in earnest, and the verse fixes its method: each line of the Navkāra is spoken (oṁ namo…) and then mentally installed on a precise body-part with a locative + sthitaṁ formula — śirasi sthitaṁ, and by parallel mukhe. Two principles of nyāsa are set here for the whole hymn. First, top-down order: the body is armoured from the crown (śiras) downward, so the Arihanta-pada — first of the Parameṣṭhins — rightly crowns the head as the śiraska (helmet), the protection of the highest, most vulnerable point. Second, fitness of element to pada: the Siddhas, bodiless and silent in perfect liberation, are placed as the mukhapaṭa over the mouth — the organ of speech and the gateway of breath. The choice is doubly apt: the muhpattī is the very emblem of guarded, ahiṁsā-bound speech, so the silent Siddhas seal the mouth that the warrior-reciter, too, may speak no harm and inhale no fault. The epithet varaṁ ('choicest') quietly marks this cloth as no ordinary gear but adamantine. Reading head-then-mouth, helmet-then-cloth, the reciter feels the protection descend pada by pada — the diamond cage taking shape from the crown down, with the two highest Parameṣṭhins guarding the two highest faculties, intellect and speech.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Navkāra's settled hierarchy — Arihanta then Siddha — and on the Jain valuation of the body's faculties. That the Siddhas, who have utterly transcended body and speech, are invoked precisely at the mouth turns the placement into a meditation on vāk-gupti (guarding of speech) and on the muhpattī's own meaning: restraint of harm to air-bodied and minute beings. Salutation (namaskāra) is treated not merely as praise but as an operative, protective force that can be 'worn'; this reflects the wider Jain conviction that the Parameṣṭhi-namaskāra is itself maṅgala and rakṣā, its merit concretely shielding the reciter.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: While chanting, the practitioner pauses at oṁ namo arihantāṇaṁ and visualizes a luminous helmet settling on the crown, then at oṁ namo siddhāṇaṁ feels a fine bright cloth drawn across the mouth — synchronizing each pada with its body-point and breath. Beyond visualization, the mouth-placement is a daily reminder to keep speech pure and non-injurious.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 1.7–1.8 (nikṣepa/nyāsa) and the concept of arhat/siddha

sources.title: Ṇamōkāra (Namokar) Mantra
sources.consultedFor: Identification of the first two padas (Namo arihantāṇaṁ, Namo siddhāṇaṁ), their antiquity (Hāthīgumphā inscription), and standard meaning.

sources.title: Arihant (Jainism)
sources.consultedFor: Meaning of arihanta as the foe-conquering, omniscient kevalin, distinct from the bodiless liberated siddha.

sources.title: Monier-Williams Sanskrit-English Dictionary (1899), Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon
sources.consultedFor: Glosses and roots for śiraska ('helmet/head-covering'), śiras, sthita (√sthā), mukha, paṭa, and vara (√vṛ).

notes: Text rendered verbatim from the book, including 'ૐ' for the Om-syllable and the daṇḍa '।' / period punctuation (punctuation marks are not glossed as lexical tokens). Both occurrences of ૐ and નમો are glossed separately per full-coverage requirement. The line is elliptical: there is no finite verb in the second hemistich, so sthitaṁ is understood by parallel from the first; '[this line] is set as…' is supplied. śiraskaṁ/mukhapaṭaṁ are accusative objects of an implied 'I place/it is placed', consistent with the hymn's nyāsa structure. Some circulating recensions read the head-line as 'śiraḥ śirasi sthitaṁ'; this edition's 'śiraskaṁ' (helmet) is followed here and is the better fit for the armour imagery. The Navkāra padas are Prakrit (Ardhamāgadhī); the placement-clauses are Sanskrit — preserved as in the source.
